meta.title: Why AI projects fail — pouk.ai
meta.description: Only 12–18% of companies deploying AI capture meaningful ROI. Here are the five failure modes — data readiness, wrong use case, integration, governance, and change management — and what to do about each.

hero.title: Why AI projects fail — and what to do about it
hero.lede: Only 12–18% of companies deploying AI are capturing meaningful ROI. The rest are stuck in one of five failure modes. Here they are — and what to do about each.

failureModesIntro.heading: Why projects fail — the five failure modes
failureModesIntro.paragraph: Most AI initiatives don't fail because the model isn't good enough. They fail before the model is ever really tested. Here's the pattern:

leaders.heading: What the leaders do differently
leaders.intro: The companies delivering the best AI returns share a pattern:

items.title: Top-down strategy
items.body: Senior leadership identifies a focused set of workflows with high economic value, then allocates resources specifically for those.

items.title: Vertical specialization
items.body: Domain-specific agents in a few key processes, not a horizontal AI layer across everything.

items.title: Measurement from day one
items.body: ROI baseline before deployment, not after; clear metrics (time saved, error reduction, revenue influenced).

items.title: Iterative rollout
items.body: Start with one team, measure, adjust, then expand.

leaders.outro: The quantified gap is significant: companies in the top quartile on AI deployment show:

whereWorks.heading: Where pouk.ai works — business knowledge meets AI tooling
whereWorks.body: The failure modes above are mostly not technical problems. They are organizational, strategic, and operational. A team that has tried a generic AI tool and been disappointed does not need a better model. They need a partner who can diagnose which failure mode the work is in and fix it — at the intersection of business knowledge and AI tooling.
whereWorks.discoveryIntro: Four questions pouk.ai walks through before recommending anything:
questions: "What specific workflow are we trying to improve, and what is the current unit cost of that workflow?"
"Who owns the data this AI would need, and what does it look like today?"
"Who inside the organization will champion this after deployment?"
"What does success look like in 90 days, and how will we measure it?"
whereWorks.closing: The diagnosis comes before the build. That is the order pouk.ai works in.
